level1_core_theme: This climactic chapter presents the culminating argument of Actor-Network Theory: sociology must undergo a Copernican revolution by placing mediators -- non-social circulating entities (law, science, religion, art, economy, politics) -- at the center of the social world, rather than treating them as objects revolving around pre-constituted social aggregates. The chapter argues that traditional sociology of the social is an object-less discipline that systematically substitutes the real beings that make people act with ersatz made of social stuff, and that a genuinely empirical social science must follow mediators themselves rather than reducing them to social explanations.

dimensions.dimension_name: The Critique of Traditional Sociological Default Settings
topics: The default setting of ignoring object-content in favor of social surroundings
Object-less discipline: sociology as a science without object
The prudishness of social explanation across knowledge domains (religion, art, science, economics)
The invention of ersatz social explanations versus taking actors seriously

concepts.definition: The pre-configured disposition in sociological inquiry that instructs: when faced with an object, ignore its content and look for the social aspects surrounding it. Latour argues this must be reversed to attend first to associations out of which objects are made, and only later to how they reshape social ties.

Latour's charge that sociology of the social has become a discipline that systematically excises non-social entities from its data, packaging only what can be rendered as social explanations. It refuses to study religion itself, objectivity itself, or attachments to goods themselves, limiting inquiry to their social dimensions alone.

Latour's metaphor for the requirement that non-social associations must be concealed under a covering of social explanations before being allowed entry into the palace of social sciences. It represents the prudish refusal to meet entities face-to-face.

knowledge_points.point: Sociology of the social systematically invents two fanciful spheres when studying religion: the believer's deluded mind and the non-existing social reality that sustains it -- all in the name of science.

Apart from religion, no domain has been more bulldozed to death by critical sociology than the sociology of art, where every artwork has been explained to nothingness by social factors hidden behind them.

The sociology of the social imposes a zero-sum game between internal (aesthetic) and external (social) explanations of art, which ANT replaces with a win/win paradigm: the more attachments and mediations, the richer the work.

dimensions.dimension_name: The Copernican Revolution and the New Topography of the Social
topics: Quasi-objects and quasi-subjects as the center of the social world
The social as provisional movement rather than domain or stuff
Collecting statements and the metrological power of traditional sociology

concepts.definition: Latour's metaphor for ANT's reorientation: instead of objects turning around social aggregates as in pre-Copernican sociology, various social aggregates emanate out of the many attachments which occupy the center of the social universe. Things, quasi-objects, and attachments are the real center -- not the agent, person, member, or society.

Entities generated by the proliferation of mediators, occupying the central position in the social universe. They are neither fully objective nor fully subjective, replacing the Kantian subject-object dichotomy with circulating hybrid entities.

In ANT, social is not a place, a thing, a domain, or a kind of stuff but a provisional movement of new associations. This change of topography eliminates both poles of the traditional explanatory framework, removing the urge for social explanation that appeals to already stabilized social ties.

Expressions (e.g., 'This is a free country!', 'the principle of precaution') that perform the social, trace new connections, and provide second-order descriptions of how social worlds should be formatted. They function as standards circulating along metrological chains and are the source of what we mean by being in a society.

The calibrated, standardized definitions of social ties maintained by the sociology of the social. Like metrology in the natural sciences, it allows other fields to operate but is not itself the source of discovery. It is only one part of the associations that make up the collective.

knowledge_points.point: Latour follows Gabriel Tarde in arguing that society is the consequence of associations (explanandum) and not their cause (explanans). The sociology of the social has confused the two.

The metrological power of the social sciences makes them excellent at calibrating stabilized definitions of the social but poor at sizing up newcomers imported during controversies. The better one is at defining the older social, the worse one is at defining the new one.

In the flattened landscape of ANT, law, science, religion, politics, and economy each circulate and associate entities in their own distinctive ways -- legal, scientific, religious, political, economic -- rather than being explained by or explaining social forces.

topics: The distinctive ways of associating (legal, scientific, religious, political, economic)
The empirical imperative: respecting what is given in experience
The ether metaphor and the liberation of non-social entities

concepts.definition: Circulating entities (law, science, religion, economy, politics, organizations, technologies, art) that gather and assemble the collective in their own distinctive ways. Unlike intermediaries that merely transmit, mediators transform, translate, and modify the associations they carry. They are 'beings out there' that must be followed if one wants to follow the actors themselves.

The ANT alternative to the zero-sum game between internal and external explanations. The more influence, mediations, and attachments, the richer the phenomenon -- contrary to the traditional view where everything gained by social explanation had to be lost by the object's inner quality.

Latour's analogy that abandoning social explanation is like abandoning the ether in physics: nothing is lost except an artifact that made impossible the development of a science by forcing observers to invent entities with contradictory features, blinding them to the real ones.

knowledge_points.point: Latour identifies at least seven distinct modes of association: law circulates to associate entities in a legal way; science ties entities together in a scientific way; religion gathers entities in a religious way; politics traces the body politic in a political way; economy provides the competence to behave economically; and so on.

The chapter ends with mediators declaring their real names: 'We are beings out there that gather and assemble the collective just as extensively as what you have called so far the social, limiting yourselves to only one standardized version of the assemblages.'

Latour insists the Copernican simile is a 'meek understatement' -- the real rupture is abandoning any sort of 'social' science unless the meaning of the adjective 'social' is permanently modified to encompass non-social circulating entities.

proper_nouns.name: Bruno Latour
proper_nouns.description: Author of Reassembling the Social and principal architect of Actor-Network Theory. In this chapter he presents the culminating argument for placing mediators at the center of social inquiry.

proper_nouns.name: Actor-Network Theory (ANT)
proper_nouns.description: The sociology of associations proposed by Latour, Callon, and Law. ANT treats the social as a provisional movement of new associations rather than a domain or substance, and insists on following non-social mediators as constitutive of the collective.

proper_nouns.name: Gabriel Tarde
proper_nouns.description: French sociologist whose distinction between explanans and explanandum is cited by Latour as foundational: society is the consequence of associations, not their cause. Tarde is positioned as the precursor to ANT against Durkheim's sociology of the social.

proper_nouns.name: Luc Boltanski and Laurent Thevenot
proper_nouns.description: French sociologists whose analysis of justifications in On Justification / Economies of Worth is cited by Latour as an example of how collecting statements format the social and provide second-order descriptions of social ordering.

proper_nouns.name: Immanuel Kant
proper_nouns.description: Referenced twice: his astronomical simile (the Copernican revolution) is borrowed and inverted by Latour; his phrase 'the sure path of science' is repurposed to describe what ANT aims to achieve for sociology.

proper_nouns.name: Sociology of the Social
proper_nouns.description: The traditional Durkheimian sociological paradigm critiqued throughout the book. It treats society as a pre-existing domain that explains action, limits inquiry to pre-defined social ties, and substitutes non-social entities with social explanations.

proper_nouns.name: Sociology of Associations
proper_nouns.description: Latour's alternative paradigm equated with ANT. It treats the social as something to be reassembled by tracing associations, follows mediators rather than reducing them, and maintains that society is the outcome rather than the cause of connections.

proper_nouns.name: Copernican Revolution (ANT metaphor)
proper_nouns.description: Latour's central metaphor for ANT's reorientation of sociology: just as Copernicus placed the Sun rather than Earth at the center, ANT places things, quasi-objects, and attachments -- not human agents or society -- at the center of the social world.

proper_nouns.name: Quasi-Objects
proper_nouns.description: Hybrid entities central to ANT, associated with Michel Serres. They are neither fully objective (objects out there) nor fully subjective (social constructs), but circulate at the center of the social universe, generated by the proliferation of mediators.

proper_nouns.name: King Midas (negative metaphor)
proper_nouns.description: Latour invokes King Midas negatively: social explanation plays the role of a negative King Midas, transforming gold, silver, and diamonds (artworks, religious experiences, scientific facts) into dust by reducing them to social factors.

proper_nouns.name: Plato's Allegory of the Cave
proper_nouns.description: Referenced by Latour to describe how critical sociology inverts Plato: objects people cherish are replaced by puppets projecting social shadows which are supposedly the only true reality behind the appreciation of the work of art.

key_arguments: Social science must reverse its default setting: attend to objects and their associations first, social ties second. When faced with an object, one should understand the associations out of which it is made before examining how it has reshaped the repertoire of social ties. This allows non-social entities to be recognized as genuine mediators rather than reduced to social proxies.

The sociology of the social is an object-less discipline -- a science without object -- that systematically substitutes the real beings actors report as making them act (gods, artworks, scientific facts) with ersatz made of social stuff. This prudishness made sense during modernization but no longer has moral, scientific, or political justification.

The Copernican revolution of ANT: things, quasi-objects, and attachments -- not human agents, nor society and its avatars -- are the real center of the social world. Social aggregates emanate from attachments, not vice versa. This is not merely a reversal of causal direction but an abolition of the two poles (explainer/explained) of traditional social explanation.

Mediators (law, science, religion, economy, politics, technology, art, organizations) each have their own irreducible way of associating entities. They are not to be explained by social forces, nor do they simply explain society in reverse. Instead, each circulates and assembles the collective in its own distinctive mode -- legal, scientific, religious, political, economic -- and their displacements trace social connections as new associations between non-social elements.

Abandoning social explanation is like abandoning the ether in physics: nothing is lost except a theoretical artifact that prevented the development of a genuine science by forcing observers to invent entities with contradictory features, blinding them to the real circulating entities that actors themselves follow. Once social explanations are relocated into the making and dissemination of standards, the other beings that gather the collective in their own ways may be emphasized at last.
